content:
# Disagreement Log — Numbers 14 (NUM_014)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### Verse 1 -- Scope of the weeping
- **EMET:** "And all the congregation lifted up and gave their voice, and the people wept in that night" -- preserves the absolute use of "lifted up" with no explicit object, followed by "gave their voice" as a separate clause; "in that night."
- **LOGOS:** "And the whole congregation lifted up their voice and cried out, and the people wept that night" -- collapses the two verbs into a hendiadys ("lifted up their voice and cried out"); "that night" without "in."
- **PNEUMA:** "And the entire community lifted up their voices and cried out, and the people wept through that night" -- uses "community" for edah, plural "voices," "through that night" implying duration.
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET's two-clause structure ("lifted up and gave their voice") as the most transparent rendering of the two Hebrew verbs (va-tissa... va-yittenu), but used "all the congregation" (with LOGOS) rather than "community" (PNEUMA) for edah. Rendered "in that night" following the Hebrew prepositional phrase ba-laylah ha-hu. The LXX adds "all" (the whole night) but the MT does not, so SOPHIA follows the MT.

### Verse 2 -- "Murmured" vs. "grumbled"; optative phrasing
- **EMET:** "And murmured... 'If only we had died... or if only... we had died!'"
- **LOGOS:** "And... murmured... 'Would that we had died... or would that we had died...'"
- **PNEUMA:** "And... grumbled... 'If only we had died... Or if only we had died...'"
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Selected "murmured" (EMET/LOGOS) over "grumbled" (PNEUMA) as the more precise rendering of va-yillonu, which is the characteristic wilderness complaint verb. Used "Would that" for the optative lu, following LOGOS, as it better captures the desiderative mood of the Hebrew particle than "If only."

### Verse 3 -- "little ones" vs. "children"; "return" vs. "go back"
- **EMET:** "Our wives and our little ones will become a plunder. Is it not better for us to return to Egypt?"
- **LOGOS:** "Our wives and our little ones will become plunder. Would it not be better for us to return to Egypt?"
- **PNEUMA:** "Our wives and children will be taken as plunder! Would it not be better for us to go back to Egypt?"
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Retained "little ones" (EMET/LOGOS) for taf, which specifically denotes small children/toddlers, not children generally. Used "return" (EMET/LOGOS) over "go back" (PNEUMA) for shuv. Also kept "And why is YHWH bringing us" with the initial conjunction (EMET/LOGOS), which PNEUMA omitted. Used "Would it not be better" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) over the more awkward "Is it not" (EMET).

### Verse 4 -- "each man to his brother" vs. "to one another"; "a head" vs. "a leader"
- **EMET:** "And said a man unto his brother: 'Let us appoint a head, and let us return to Egypt.'"
- **LOGOS:** "And they said, each man to his brother, 'Let us appoint a head and let us return to Egypt.'"
- **PNEUMA:** "And they said to one another, 'Let us appoint a leader and go back to Egypt.'"
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "each man to his brother" (LOGOS), which preserves the Hebrew idiom ish el akhiv while being readable English. Retained "a head" (EMET/LOGOS) over "a leader" (PNEUMA) for rosh, as "head" is the literal Hebrew and carries the connotation of replacing Moses specifically. Used "let us return" (EMET/LOGOS) over "go back" (PNEUMA).

### Verse 6 -- "from those who had spied out" vs. "who were among those who had scouted"
- **EMET:** "from the ones spying out the land" -- present participle, literal rendering of ha-tarim.
- **LOGOS:** "who were among those who had spied out the land" -- past tense, explanatory.
- **PNEUMA:** "who were among those who had scouted the land" -- uses "scouted" for tur.
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "from those who had spied out the land" as a compromise: "from" follows EMET's rendering of the preposition min; "had spied out" follows LOGOS's past tense as more natural in English narrative; "spied out" (EMET/LOGOS) over "scouted" (PNEUMA) for tur, as "spy out" is the traditional and more precise rendering.

### Verse 7 -- "exceedingly, exceedingly good" vs. "exceedingly good, very much so" vs. "surpassingly, surpassingly good"
- **EMET:** "good the land, exceedingly, exceedingly" -- word-order follows Hebrew (predicate-subject).
- **LOGOS:** "the land is exceedingly good, very much so" -- English word order, breaks up the doubled me'od.
- **PNEUMA:** "surpassingly, surpassingly good" -- doubles "surpassingly" to capture me'od me'od.
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Rendered "the land is exceedingly, exceedingly good" -- uses natural English word order (subject-verb) but preserves the doubled me'od me'od as "exceedingly, exceedingly" to reflect the Hebrew superlative. This captures the emphatic doubling that LOGOS flattened and uses the standard "exceedingly" rather than PNEUMA's less conventional "surpassingly."

### Verse 8 -- Conditional structure
- **EMET:** "If YHWH delights in us, and he will bring us unto this land, and give it to us -- a land which it is flowing with milk and honey."
- **LOGOS:** "If YHWH delights in us, then he will bring us into this land and give it to us, a land which flows with milk and honey."
- **PNEUMA:** "If YHWH delights in us, he will bring us into this land and give it to us -- a land flowing with milk and honey."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "then he will bring us" for clarity of the conditional structure. Used "a land which flows with milk and honey" (LOGOS) as a compromise between EMET's over-literal "which it is flowing" and PNEUMA's compressed "a land flowing."

### Verse 9 -- "shade" phrasing; "bread for us" vs. "our bread"
- **EMET:** "for our bread they are. Their shade has turned away from upon them"
- **LOGOS:** "for they are our bread. Their shade has departed from upon them"
- **PNEUMA:** "they are bread for us! Their shade of protection has departed from over them"
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "for they are our bread" (LOGOS) as the most natural English rendering of ki lakh-menu hem. Used "Their shade has departed from upon them" (LOGOS) -- "departed" is slightly more natural than EMET's "turned away" for sar, and avoids PNEUMA's interpretive addition "of protection." The Hebrew tsilam ("their shade") is itself the metaphor; adding "of protection" is commentary, not translation.

### Verse 11 -- "despite" vs. "with all the signs"
- **EMET:** "will they not believe in me with all the signs which I have done in their midst?"
- **LOGOS:** "will they not believe in me, with all the signs which I have performed in their midst?"
- **PNEUMA:** "will they refuse to trust me, despite all the signs I have performed in their midst?"
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "despite" (PNEUMA) for the preposition be-khol in this context, as it better captures the concessive force -- the signs should have produced belief but did not. However, used "believe in me" (EMET/LOGOS) rather than "trust me" (PNEUMA) for ya'aminu bi, preserving the theologically loaded verb. Used "performed" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) over "done" (EMET) for asiti.

### Verse 12 -- "pestilence" vs. "plague"
- **EMET:** "I will strike them with the pestilence and I will dispossess them"
- **LOGOS:** "I will strike them with pestilence and dispossess them"
- **PNEUMA:** "I will strike them with a plague and dispossess them"
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "pestilence" (EMET/LOGOS) for dever, as this is the standard English rendering of this specific Hebrew term and distinguishes it from maggefah ("plague") which appears in v. 37. The distinction matters: dever is a particular kind of epidemic; maggefah is a divine striking-blow.

### Verse 13 -- "Egypt will hear" vs. "the Egyptians will hear"
- **EMET:** "And will hear Egypt -- for you brought up in your strength this people from their midst."
- **LOGOS:** "Then the Egyptians will hear -- for you brought up this people in your might from their midst."
- **PNEUMA:** "But the Egyptians will hear of it! For you brought this people up from among them by your power."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "Then Egypt will hear of it" -- "Egypt" (EMET) rather than "the Egyptians" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for Mitsrayim, which is the place-name used metonymically, not the gentillic. Added "of it" (PNEUMA) for English fluency. Used "by your power" following PNEUMA's natural phrasing but rendered be-kokhakha faithfully.

### Verse 14 -- Structure and phrasing of the nations' knowledge
- **EMET:** Highly literal, interlinear style with brackets.
- **LOGOS:** "and they will tell it to the inhabitants of this land. They have heard that you, YHWH, are in the midst of this people, that you, YHWH, are seen eye to eye..."
- **PNEUMA:** "and they will tell it to the inhabitants of this land. They have heard that you, YHWH, are in the midst of this people -- that you, YHWH, are seen eye to eye, that your cloud stands over them, and that you go before them..."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS's structure, which is close to PNEUMA's but uses slightly more formal connectives. SOPHIA's rendering follows LOGOS almost verbatim here as the best balance of accuracy and readability. The "that... that..." clause structure (PNEUMA) was adopted for clarity of the subordinate clauses.

### Verse 15 -- "this people" vs. "this entire people"; "report of you" vs. "your fame"
- **EMET:** "And you will put to death this people as one man, and will say the nations who have heard your report, saying:"
- **LOGOS:** "And if you put this people to death as one man, then the nations who have heard the report of you will say,"
- **PNEUMA:** "And if you put this entire people to death as one man, then the nations who have heard your fame will say,"
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "your fame" (PNEUMA) for shim-akha, as this word (from the root shama, "to hear") means "what is heard about you" -- i.e., reputation/fame, not merely a single report. However, retained "this people" (EMET/LOGOS) without PNEUMA's addition of "entire," which is not in the Hebrew.

### Verse 16 -- "which he swore to them" vs. "he swore to give them"
- **EMET:** "the land which he swore to them, and he slaughtered them in the wilderness."
- **LOGOS:** "the land which he swore to them, he slaughtered them in the wilderness."
- **PNEUMA:** "the land he swore to give them, he slaughtered them in the wilderness."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "which he swore to them" (EMET/LOGOS). PNEUMA adds "to give" which is implicit but not in the Hebrew text of this verse. The MT reads simply nishba lahem ("he swore to them").

### Verse 17 -- Inclusion of "please" (na)
- **EMET:** "And now, let be great, please, the power of my Lord, as you spoke, saying:"
- **LOGOS:** "And now, let the power of my Lord be great, just as you have spoken, saying,"
- **PNEUMA:** "And now, please, let the power of my Lord be great, just as you declared when you said,"
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Included "please" (EMET/PNEUMA) for the particle na, which LOGOS omitted. Positioned it after "be great" following Hebrew word order more closely: "let the power of my Lord be great, please." Used "just as you spoke" (LOGOS) rather than "just as you declared when you said" (PNEUMA), which is over-translated.

### Verse 18 -- "bearing" vs. "forgiving"; infinitive absolute; "fathers/sons" vs. "parents/children"
- **EMET:** "bearing iniquity and transgression, and acquitting -- he will not acquit, visiting the iniquity of fathers upon sons, upon the third and upon the fourth generation."
- **LOGOS:** "forgiving iniquity and transgression, but he will by no means leave the guilty unpunished, visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the sons, upon the third and upon the fourth generation."
- **PNEUMA:** "bearing wrongdoing and rebellion -- yet he will certainly not leave the guilty unpunished, visiting the consequences of the parents' wrongdoing on their children to the third and fourth generation."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "bearing iniquity and transgression" -- "bearing" (EMET/PNEUMA) for nose rather than "forgiving" (LOGOS), as nasa literally means "to bear/carry" and the forensic sense of carrying away sin is primary. Used "iniquity and transgression" (EMET/LOGOS) over "wrongdoing and rebellion" (PNEUMA) for avon and pesha. For the infinitive absolute ve-naqqeh lo yenaqqeh, rendered "yet acquitting, he will not acquit" (close to EMET) to preserve the infinitive absolute doubling per the binding conventions. LOGOS's "by no means leave the guilty unpunished" and PNEUMA's "certainly not leave the guilty unpunished" are idiomatic but lose the doubling structure. Used "fathers... sons" (EMET/LOGOS) over "parents... children" (PNEUMA).

### Verse 19 -- "Forgive" vs. "Pardon"; "borne" vs. "forgiven" vs. "carried"
- **EMET:** "Forgive, please, the iniquity of this people according to the greatness of your steadfast love, and as you have borne this people from Egypt and until here."
- **LOGOS:** "Pardon, I pray, the iniquity of this people according to the greatness of your steadfast love, and just as you have forgiven this people from Egypt even until now."
- **PNEUMA:** "Please forgive the wrongdoing of this people, according to the greatness of your faithful love, just as you have carried this people from Egypt until now."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "Forgive, please" (EMET) for selakh na -- "forgive" is the most direct rendering of salakh, and "please" for na is positioned after the imperative as in Hebrew. Used "iniquity" (EMET/LOGOS) over "wrongdoing" (PNEUMA) for avon. Used "steadfast love" (EMET/LOGOS) over "faithful love" (PNEUMA) for khesed, as "steadfast love" is the established standard rendering. Used "borne" for nasatah -- this preserves the double meaning of nasa (to bear/carry AND to forgive) that both "forgiven" (LOGOS) and "carried" (PNEUMA) lose by choosing only one sense.

### Verse 21 -- "But" vs. "And yet"
- **EMET:** "And yet, as I live, and fills the glory of YHWH all the earth --"
- **LOGOS:** "But as I live, and as all the earth shall be filled with the glory of YHWH,"
- **PNEUMA:** "But as surely as I live, and as all the earth will be filled with the glory of YHWH --"
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "But as I live" (LOGOS) -- "but" captures the adversative ve-ulam adequately, and "as I live" is more natural than EMET's literal rendering. Used "and as all the earth shall be filled" (LOGOS), which is more formally appropriate for the oath register than PNEUMA's "will be."

### Verse 23 -- Oath idiom rendering
- **EMET:** "If they shall see the land which I swore to their fathers; and all the ones despising me shall not see it."
- **LOGOS:** "they shall not see the land which I swore to their fathers. And none of those who despised me shall see it."
- **PNEUMA:** "they will never see the land I swore to their fathers. And none who despised me will see it."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Rendered the negative oath formula im yir'u as "they shall not see" (LOGOS) rather than preserving the raw Hebrew "if they shall see" (EMET), which is unintelligible to English readers, or "never" (PNEUMA), which over-translates. Used "none who despised me" (PNEUMA, slightly compressed) for mena'atsai.

### Verse 24 -- "seed" vs. "descendants"
- **EMET:** "and his seed shall possess it."
- **LOGOS:** "and his seed shall possess it."
- **PNEUMA:** "and his descendants will possess it."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "seed" (EMET/LOGOS) for zar'o, which preserves the biblical terminology and its resonance with the patriarchal promises. Two-to-one majority also favors this.

### Verse 25 -- Singular vs. plural for the nations
- **EMET:** "And the Amalekite and the Canaanite are dwelling in the valley."
- **LOGOS:** "Now the Amalekite and the Canaanite dwell in the valley."
- **PNEUMA:** "Now the Amalekites and the Canaanites dwell in the valley."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used singular "the Amalekite and the Canaanite" (EMET/LOGOS), which follows the Hebrew collective singular yoshev. PNEUMA's plurals are interpretive.

### Verse 27 -- "evil congregation" vs. "wicked community"; murmuring clause structure
- **EMET:** "Until when for this evil congregation -- who they are murmuring against me? The murmurings of the Sons of Israel which they are murmuring against me, I have heard."
- **LOGOS:** "How long shall I bear with this evil congregation who murmur against me? The murmurings of the Sons of Israel, which they murmur against me, I have heard."
- **PNEUMA:** "How long shall I bear with this wicked community that grumbles against me? I have heard the complaints that the Sons of Israel are raising against me."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS's rendering as the clearest formal equivalent. Used "evil congregation" (EMET/LOGOS) for ha-edah ha-ra'ah over "wicked community" (PNEUMA). Used "murmur" / "murmurings" (EMET/LOGOS) for mallinim / telunnot rather than "grumbles" / "complaints" (PNEUMA), preserving the cognate relationship between the verb and noun. Retained the MT word order with "I have heard" at the end (EMET/LOGOS).

### Verse 28 -- Oath formula rendering
- **EMET:** "'As I live -- the utterance of YHWH -- if not as you spoke in my ears, so I will do to you:"
- **LOGOS:** "'As I live -- declares YHWH -- just as you have spoken in my ears, so I will do to you."
- **PNEUMA:** "'As surely as I live' -- declares YHWH -- 'just as you have spoken in my hearing, so I will do to you."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "As I live -- declares YHWH -- surely as you have spoken in my ears, so I will do to you." Rendered the positive oath formula im lo as "surely" (converting the Hebrew idiom to English), following LOGOS's approach. Used "declares" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for ne'um over "the utterance of" (EMET). Kept "in my ears" (EMET/LOGOS) over "in my hearing" (PNEUMA) for be-oznai.

### Verse 29 -- "carcasses" vs. "corpses"
- **EMET:** "shall fall your corpses"
- **LOGOS:** "your carcasses shall fall"
- **PNEUMA:** "your corpses will fall"
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "carcasses" (LOGOS) for pigreikem. While both "corpses" and "carcasses" are valid, "carcasses" better captures the degrading connotation of peger, which implies unburied bodies left to rot -- a term more commonly used for animal remains, emphasizing the humiliation of the judgment.

### Verse 30 -- "lifted my hand" vs. "raised my hand"
- **EMET:** "which I lifted my hand to make you dwell in it"
- **LOGOS:** "in which I lifted my hand to settle you"
- **PNEUMA:** "where I raised my hand to settle you"
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "raised my hand" (PNEUMA) for nasati et yadi, which is slightly more natural English for the oath gesture, combined with "to settle you" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for le-shakkein etkhem.

### Verse 31 -- Minor structural differences
- **EMET:** "and I will bring them, and they shall know the land which you rejected"
- **LOGOS:** "I will bring them in, and they shall know the land which you have rejected."
- **PNEUMA:** "I will bring them in, and they will know the land that you rejected."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "I will bring them in, and they shall know the land which you have rejected" (LOGOS), adopting "bring them in" and "shall" for the more formal register appropriate to divine speech.

### Verse 33 -- "shepherds" vs. "wanderers"; "whoredoms/harlotries" vs. "unfaithfulness"
- **EMET:** "And your sons shall be shepherds in the wilderness forty years, and they shall bear your whoredoms until the completion of your corpses in the wilderness."
- **LOGOS:** "And your sons shall be shepherds in the wilderness forty years, and they shall bear your harlotries until your carcasses are consumed in the wilderness."
- **PNEUMA:** "And your children will be wanderers in the wilderness for forty years, bearing the burden of your unfaithfulness, until the last of your corpses lies in the wilderness."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "sons" (EMET/LOGOS) for beneikhem over "children" (PNEUMA). Used "shepherds" (EMET/LOGOS) for ro'im, which is the literal meaning -- PNEUMA's "wanderers" is interpretive. For zenuteikem, used "unfaithfulness" as a compromise: "whoredoms" (EMET) and "harlotries" (LOGOS) are literal but archaic; PNEUMA's "unfaithfulness" captures the metaphorical sense while remaining accessible. Used "until your carcasses are consumed" (LOGOS) for ad tom pigreikem, which is clearer than EMET's "until the completion of" and more accurate than PNEUMA's paraphrase.

### Verse 34 -- "my opposition" vs. "what it means to have me against you"
- **EMET:** "and you shall know my opposition."
- **LOGOS:** "and you shall know my opposition."
- **PNEUMA:** "and you will know what it means to have me against you."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "my opposition" (EMET/LOGOS) for tenu'ati. This rare word (hapax in the Pentateuch) derives from the root nu ("to hinder/oppose"). PNEUMA's paraphrase is vivid but too expansive for a translation. Two-to-one majority supports "my opposition."

### Verse 35 -- "Surely this I will do" vs. "I will surely do this"
- **EMET:** "if not this I will do to all this evil congregation, the ones assembled against me: in this wilderness they shall be finished, and there they shall die."
- **LOGOS:** "Surely this I will do to all this evil congregation who have gathered together against me. In this wilderness they shall be consumed, and there they shall die."
- **PNEUMA:** "I will surely do this to this entire wicked community that has banded together against me. In this wilderness they will be spent. And there they will die."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS: "Surely this I will do..." -- LOGOS's word order is more emphatic and closer to the Hebrew (zot e'eseh). Used "gathered together" (LOGOS) over "banded together" (PNEUMA) for ha-no'adim. Used "consumed" (LOGOS) for yittammu over "finished" (EMET) or "spent" (PNEUMA). Used "evil congregation" (EMET/LOGOS) consistently.

### Verse 37 -- "died by the plague" vs. "died by plague"
- **EMET:** "died by the plague before YHWH" (with definite article).
- **LOGOS:** "died by the plague before YHWH" (with definite article).
- **PNEUMA:** "died by plague before YHWH" (without definite article).
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "by the plague" (EMET/LOGOS), following the Hebrew ba-maggefah which has the definite article.

### Verse 39 -- "mourned greatly" vs. "mourned exceedingly" vs. "mourned deeply"
- **EMET:** "and mourned the people exceedingly."
- **LOGOS:** "and the people mourned greatly."
- **PNEUMA:** "and the people mourned deeply."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "mourned greatly" (LOGOS) for va-yit'abbelu... me'od. "Greatly" is the most standard English rendering of me'od in narrative contexts.

### Verse 40 -- "top of the mountain" vs. "heights of the hill country"
- **EMET:** "and went up unto the top of the mountain"
- **LOGOS:** "and went up to the top of the mountain"
- **PNEUMA:** "and went up to the heights of the hill country"
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "the top of the mountain" (EMET/LOGOS) for rosh ha-har, which is the literal rendering. PNEUMA's "heights of the hill country" is an interpretation appropriate for v. 44-45 context but not what the Hebrew says here.

### Verse 41 -- "the mouth of YHWH" vs. "the command of YHWH"
- **EMET:** "you are transgressing the mouth of YHWH"
- **LOGOS:** "Why now do you transgress the mouth of YHWH?"
- **PNEUMA:** "Why now are you transgressing the command of YHWH?"
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "the command of YHWH" (PNEUMA) for et pi YHWH. While "the mouth of YHWH" (EMET/LOGOS) is literal, the Hebrew idiom pi YHWH means "YHWH's spoken command" -- the metonymy (mouth = command) is standard and rendering it as "command" is defensible. However, this was a close call; SOPHIA chose clarity for the English reader while noting the literal reading in the disagreement.

### Verse 42 -- "lest you be struck down" vs. "and do not be struck"
- **EMET:** "and do not be struck before your enemies."
- **LOGOS:** "lest you be struck down before your enemies."
- **PNEUMA:** "or you will be struck down before your enemies."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "lest you be struck down" (LOGOS) for ve-lo tinnagfu. The conjunction ve-lo following the prohibition functions as a purpose/result clause ("lest"), which LOGOS captures most accurately. EMET reads it as a second prohibition; PNEUMA reads it as a consequence.

### Verse 43 -- "Because you have turned back from following YHWH"
- **EMET:** "because you turned back from after YHWH"
- **LOGOS:** "Because you have turned back from following YHWH"
- **PNEUMA:** "Because you have turned back from following YHWH"
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "from following YHWH" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) rather than "from after YHWH" (EMET) for me-akharei YHWH, as "following" makes the idiomatic sense clear in English.

### Verse 44 -- "But they presumed" vs. "Yet in their presumption they surged up"
- **EMET:** "And they presumed to go up unto the top of the mountain; and the ark of the covenant of YHWH and Moses did not move from the midst of the camp."
- **LOGOS:** "But they presumed to go up to the top of the mountain. And the ark of the covenant of YHWH and Moses did not depart from the midst of the camp."
- **PNEUMA:** "Yet in their presumption they surged up toward the heights of the hill country, even though neither the Ark of the Covenant of YHWH nor Moses moved from the camp."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "But they presumed to go up" (LOGOS) for va-ya'pilu la-alot, preserving the rare verb as a straightforward English equivalent. PNEUMA's "surged up" is too free. Used "though" rather than "and" for the second clause to capture the concessive force, and "did not depart from the midst of the camp" (LOGOS) for accuracy.

### Verse 45 -- "struck them and crushed them" vs. "struck them and beat them down"
- **EMET:** "and struck them and crushed them until the Hormah."
- **LOGOS:** "and struck them and beat them down as far as Hormah."
- **PNEUMA:** "and struck them and crushed them, all the way to Hormah."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "struck them and crushed them as far as Hormah." Took "crushed" (EMET/PNEUMA) for va-yakketu'um from the root katat (to crush/shatter), which is more severe and accurate than "beat them down" (LOGOS). Used "as far as" (LOGOS) over "until the" (EMET) or "all the way to" (PNEUMA). Rendered "Hormah" without the definite article, as English convention treats it as a proper noun, though the Hebrew has ha-Khormah.

---

*-- SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter*
